topics.title: Java Serialization & Externalization
topics.description: Serialization converts a Java object graph into a byte stream for storage or transmission; deserialization reverses it. Implementing Serializable marks a class as eligible. serialVersionUID prevents version mismatch. Externalizable gives full manual control. Transient fields are skipped. Security pitfall: Java deserialization of untrusted data can execute arbitrary code — always prefer JSON/Protobuf over native serialization for external data.
topics.story: Imagine you're moving house (serialization). You pack every piece of furniture into labeled boxes (byte stream). When you arrive at the new house (JVM on another machine), you unpack in the same order. But if you've renovated (changed the class) since packing, the furniture might not fit the new rooms — that's a serialVersionUID mismatch. Externalizable is like packing yourself with total control over what goes in each box.
topics.analogy: Serializable = auto-packer: Java decides what goes in the boxes. transient = 'do not pack this item' sticker. serialVersionUID = version label on the box — receiver rejects boxes with the wrong version. Externalizable = custom packing: you write writeExternal() and readExternal() yourself. ObjectOutputStream = the moving truck. ObjectInputStream = the unpacking crew.
topics.memoryAnchor: implements Serializable (marker interface). serialVersionUID: private static final long. transient: skip field. ObjectOutputStream.writeObject() / ObjectInputStream.readObject(). Externalizable: writeExternal(ObjectOutput) + readExternal(ObjectInput). readResolve() for singleton. serialization of subclass: parent needs no-arg constructor if not Serializable. Jackson/Gson preferred for JSON instead of Java serialization for cross-language/security.
topics.firstPrinciples: Java serialization uses reflection to traverse the object graph and writes field values in declaration order. serialVersionUID is a hash of class structure — if omitted, JVM auto-generates it, changing when you add/remove fields. Deserialization constructs objects bypassing constructors — this is why transient initialization logic in constructors won't run on deserialization. Externalizable's readExternal() calls the public no-arg constructor first, then populates fields.
topics.bruteForce: Manual serialization: convert each field to String, write to file with delimiters. Parse back on read. Error-prone for nested objects, collections, inheritance, null values, and schema evolution.
topics.optimizedApproach: Java serialization: implement Serializable, declare serialVersionUID, mark sensitive/derived fields transient. For cross-language or external APIs: use Jackson (JSON) or Protobuf. For performance-critical caching: Kryo or FST. Always validate deserialized objects — never deserialize from untrusted sources using native Java serialization.
topics.whenToUse: Java serialization: Java-to-Java communication (RMI, JMS), Java caching (Ehcache, Hazelcast). Avoid for REST APIs (use Jackson), avoid for untrusted input (security). Externalizable: when you need precise control over format or maximum performance. transient: passwords, computed fields, non-serializable references (Logger, Thread, Socket).

examples.title: Basic Serialization with serialVersionUID & transient
examples.explanation: serialVersionUID = 1L is a stable version contract. If you add fields later, the old serialized data still deserializes (new fields get default values). Without serialVersionUID, Java auto-computes it from the class signature — adding any field changes it and breaks deserialization. transient fields are null/0 after deserialization. readObject() is the hook to reinitialize derived state.
examples.realWorldUse: HTTP session replication in clustered Tomcat uses Java serialization — always mark Logger, service references transient. Distributed caches (Hazelcast, Redis with Kryo) require Serializable for stored POJOs. DevLearner: UserTopicProgress stored in DB as JSON — Jackson serialization, not Java serialization.

examples.title: Externalizable — Full Manual Control
examples.explanation: Externalizable requires public no-arg constructor — Java calls it before readExternal(). You write and read fields in the exact same order. Only write what you need — great for reducing payload size (skip large description). readResolve() is the serialization hook for Singleton — return the existing instance instead of the deserialized one, preserving the single-instance contract.
examples.realWorldUse: High-throughput message queues (Kafka, JMS) use Externalizable or Kryo for compact serialization. Singleton services (config managers, registry) use readResolve() to prevent a deserialization attack creating second instances. In microservices, Protobuf/Avro replaces Java serialization for cross-service contracts.

problems.title: Fix the Serialization Bug
problems.hint1: Add a fixed serialVersionUID to prevent auto-generated version mismatches.
problems.hint2: Logger and Connection cannot be serialized — mark them transient.
problems.hint3: transient fields are null after deserialization — reinitialize them in readObject() if needed.

problems.title: Deep Clone via Serialization
problems.description: Implement a generic `deepClone(T obj)` method that uses serialization to create a completely independent copy of an object graph (not just shallow copy).
problems.hint1: Serialize the object to a ByteArrayOutputStream, then deserialize from ByteArrayInputStream.
problems.hint2: The returned object is a completely independent copy — changes to nested objects won't affect the original.
problems.hint3: The type T must implement Serializable for this to work.
problems.testCases:
Original: [a, b, c]
Clone:    [a, b, c, d]

problems.title: Implement Externalizable for Compact Serialization
problems.description: A `Point3D` class has x, y, z (double) and a label (String). Using Externalizable, serialize only x, y, z (skip label). Verify label is null after deserialization and the coordinates are preserved.
problems.hint1: Implement Externalizable (not Serializable). Add a public no-arg constructor.
problems.hint2: writeExternal: write x, y, z with out.writeDouble(). readExternal: read in same order.
problems.hint3: label is not written — it will be null after deserialization.
